EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain in the late afternoon changed to snow in the early evening, with the snow accumulating 2 to 4 inches. Icy roads from the freezing rain resulted in several accidents with no reported injuries in the South Sioux City area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving upper level system moved from the southwestern United States across the plains tapping gulf moisture as it moved near eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  With Arctic air in place across the area...the moisture and forcing from the system produced freezing rain and sleet over southeast and east central Nebraska and southwest Iowa and heavy snow over northeast Nebraska and west central Iowa.  Freezing rain accumulations of 1/4 to 1/3 inch were common from far southeast Nebraska into far southwest Iowa, with 1/2 to 3/4 inch sleet mixed with freezing rain accumulations common just north of there including the Lincoln and Omaha areas.  Farther north and northwest the precipitation was mostly snow...although some sleet also fell...with 6 to 11 inches of snow reported along and northeast of a Dodge to Norfolk to Neligh line.  Some of the heavier snow totals included around 11 inches at Walthill and near Battle Creek and around 10 inches near Norfolk and Wayne.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow spread across Wayne county during the evening hours of 12/18.  Snowfall of an inch or two per hour fell for a few hours during the evening and early overnight hours, and much of the county likely recorded 8 to 10 inches of snow.  Wayne measured 10 inches from the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of snow produced 6 to 10 inches across sections of northeast Nebraska, mainly west through north of Neligh.  The snow began Sunday morning January 20th and lasted into Monday evening the 21st.  Higher storm totals included between 10 and 11 inches at Orchard and around 8.5 inches in Bloomfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of between 10 and 11 inches was reported over the western sections of Antelope county over a 30 to 36 hour period ending late Monday evening January 21st.  Orchard reported 9.5 inches of snow by 800 am on the 21st, with another 1 to 1.5 inches estimated to have fallen later that day.  Neligh, farther to the southeast, reported just under 6 inches for the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow accompanied by 40-50 mph winds resulted in whiteout conditions for a time across the Tri-State region during the day. Snowfall totals ranged from one to two inches while visibilities of near zero to 1/4 mile in falling and blowing snow occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility was reported to be less than one quarter of a mile with moderate snow.  Snow accumulations of 1 to 3 inches occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Canadian air mass plunged southeast into the Central Plains during the late morning and afternoon hours of October 26th as temperatures in the 30s and 40s replaced 60s to around 70 which were observed the previous afternoon.  The cold air was ushered into the region on northwest winds that were sustained at 30 to 35 mph with gusts of 45 to 55 mph.  A few wind gusts reached high wind criteria of 58 mph or greater for a brief period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts reaching 59 mph were measured by Dept. of Roads wind equipment near Cedar Rapids.  Wind gusts of this magnitude were recorded for around a 3 hour period from 1pm through 4pm CDT.  The winds were the result of Canadian air spilling southeast across the plains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snowfall combined with strong winds to produce significant blowing snow.  Peak wind gust around 65 mph recorded at Sidney and Dalton, Nebraska.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area moved northeast out of Colorado during the day.  Severe thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon hours and continued well into the evening. Nearly 50 reports of severe weather were received in NW Kansas with a few hail reports in SW Nebraska.

A surface low pressure area moved northeast out of Colorado during the day.  Severe thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon hours and continued well into the evening. Nearly 50 reports of severe weather were received in NW Kansas with a few hail reports in SW Nebraska.

Southern Nebraska was on the fringe of the major tornado event in NW Kansas on this day.  One long-track tornado moved across most of Decatur county Kansas before entering Red Willow county and producing damage. An isolated hail report was also received.

A series of severe thunderstorms developed in a SW-NE line and moved northeast, producing repeated heavy rain and hail episodes from northern Yuma County Colorado eastward across Dundy and Hitchcock counties in Nebraska. Several flash flood reports, and dozens of large hail reports were received.  Several brief tornadoes were also reported, but no damage was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was mostly one inch in diameter with a few golf ball sized stones. Hail lasted for almost 25 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms moved across southwest Nebraska during the early morning hours producing heavy rainfall and strong winds. An intense thunderstorm outflow produced damage to a home in northern Dundy county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home lost about 20% of its roof, and five utility poles nearby were broken by a strong thunderstorm downburst.  A patio fence was damaged and the last section of an irrigation pivot were blown over.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm spotter reported hail covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours, a line of thunderstorms developed over northeast Colorado producing a brief landspout tornado 3 miles northwest of Yuma. This line of storms went on to produce wind gusts approaching 60 mph in northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at McCook measured at 50 knot wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of severe thunderstorms developed in a SW-NE line and moved northeast, producing repeated heavy rain and hail episodes from northern Yuma County Colorado eastward across Dundy and Hitchcock counties in Nebraska. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm covered south central Nebraska with a fresh blanket of 6 inches of snow on average. The snow rolled in around sunrise the morning of the 5th and continued into the evening hours. A wide swath of 5 to 8 inches of snow fell along U.S. Highway 6 and 34 from Arapahoe to Hastings, and then over toward Aurora and York. The National Weather Service Office north of Hastings measured 7.7 inches of new snow. Cambridge had 7.4 inches and Holdrege 7 inches.  There was some blowing and drifting reported.  Many, if not most, schools were closed at least one day due to the winter storm conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As much as 6 inches of snow fell in parts of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours, a complex of thunderstorms developed over northeast Colorado and southwest Nebraska. These storms moved to the southeast producing a brief tornado northwest of McCook, NE. Dime to tennis ball size hail was reported over eastern Colorado and northwest Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement officials reported hail covered the ground one inch deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours, a complex of storms developed over eastern Colorado producing wind gusts in excess of 60 mph. The storms then moved into southwest Nebraska and northwest Kansas where a wind gust of 67 mph was measured in Colby, KS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported at Trenton Dam.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cooperative observer reported tree limbs up to 6 inches in diameter were broken off around town. Also, a camper was reported to have been blown over and a privacy fence destroyed by the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed over southwest Nebraska and northwest Kansas producing a brief tornado in Sheridan County, KS as well as isolated severe wind and hail reports.
A strong upper level system moved from Wyoming into South Dakota bringing heavy snow to portions of North Central Nebraska during the night of March 30th through the morning of March 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow occurred across Keya Paha County during the night of March 30th into the morning of March 31st.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 5 to 8 inches over far northeast Nebraska during a 24 hour period from the afternoon of January 20th to the afternoon of January 21st. The snowfall made travel slippery.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 8 inches made travel slippery, with several vehicles sliding off roads. The 8 inch accumulations were in the northern part of the county near the Missouri River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 3 to 6 inches over extreme northeast Nebraska during a 17 hour period from the morning of February 11th into the night. The snowfall made travel slippery, but it was not accompanied by freezing rain or significant blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 3 to 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow-melt flooding was experienced on the North Fork of the Elkhorn River for a few days.  The River at Pierce crested at 12.7 feet at 10 pm CST on March 3rd, flood stage is 12 feet.  The river at Pierce was above flood stage from 11 am on March 2nd until 645 am on the 4th.  Flooding along Elkhorn crossed around 25 county roads near Pierce, closing many of them for a while.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The flooding crossed county roads around 25 times near Pierce, closing many of them for a time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several ice jams from around West Point in Cuming county downstream to near Hooper and Winslow caused flooding of a few county roads and agricultural lowlands along the river.  The flooding also closed Highway 275 near Crowell for a while.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters from the Elkhorn River due to several ice jams closed Highway 275 near Crowell for a few days and also flooded agricultural lowlands and a few county roads near Crowell and also around Hooper and Winslow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning thunderstorms developed north of a warm front that was located across northeast Kansas.  The storms were fueled by a low level jet and produced isolated reports of large hail in southeast Nebraska.

Low pressure that tracked across northeast Kansas brought heavy snow to sections of extreme northeast Nebraska.  Crofton and Bloomfield in Knox county received around 6 inches of wet snow from this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crofton and Bloomfield reported around 6 inches of wet snow from this storm.  Some schools in the region were closed because of the slushy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching upper level disturbance from the Central Rockies, lifted across Southwestern Nebraska during the late afternoon hours of April 23rd.  As this feature combined with surface dew points in the 50s and a stationary front anchored across Nebraska, numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed across Southwestern, Central and portions of North Central Nebraska.  Activity began around 5 PM CDT and ended by 8 PM CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought 5-6 inches of snow to parts of the Tri-State area, accompanied by winds of 40-50 mph.  The combination of snow and wind resulted in jack-knifed trailers and several vehicle accidents. Some state and county roads were closed for a short period of time due to minor accidents, drifting and reduced visibility in falling and blowing snow. In Nebraska, snow totals were:  Dundy county 4-6 inches, Hitchcock county 3-5 inches and in Red Willow county 2-3 inches. Peak winds in all three counties were 40-45 mph for much of the day.

Severe thunderstorms developed with the heating of the day as a warm front lifted north across Nebraska. Previous days of rainfall resulted in an increased potential for flash flooding with a Flood Watch issued at 805 AM CST for portions of North Central and Southwest Nebraska. At 133 PM CST the Storms Prediction Center issued Tornado Watch number 384 that ran until 8 PM CST. Severe thunderstorms developed that led to numerous large hail reports in southwest and north central Nebraska. In addition, flash flooding occurred in Frontier county.

A very strong and unseasonably cold upper level low pressure system that was tracking across the northern plains brought a strong low level jet to the region during the early morning hours of June 11th.  The warm and unstable air that worked north into the region helped spawn early morning severe thunderstorms across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near the intersection of 120th and Church.

As this feature combined with surface dew points in the 50s and a stationary front anchored across Nebraska, numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed across Southwestern, Central and portions of North Central Nebraska.  Activity began around 5 PM CDT and ended by 8 PM CDT.

The warm and unstable air that worked north into the region helped spawn early morning severe thunderstorms across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed with the heating of the day as a warm front lifted north across Nebraska. Previous days of rainfall resulted in an increased potential for flash flooding with a Flood Watch issued at 805 AM CST for portions of North Central and Southwest Nebraska. At 133 PM CST the Storms Prediction Center issued Tornado Watch number 384 that ran until 8 PM CST. Severe thunderstorms developed that led to numerous large hail reports in southwest and north central Nebraska. In addition, flash flooding occurred in Frontier county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms produced some hail and flooding over parts of eastern Cheyenne county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail from 3/4 inch to 1 inch in diameter reported along track of storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms produced some hail and flooding over parts of eastern Cheyenne county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water up to 2 1/2 feet in depth reported on U.S. Highway 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced some large hail over eastern Dawes county, NE.
Thunderstorms produced some strong outflow winds over parts of Morrill county, causing some downing of tree branches.
A severe thunderstorm produced large hail over parts of Scotts Bluff county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many reports of hail from 1.00 to around 1.75 inches in diameter reported between Mitchell and Scottsbluff, Nebraska.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced some hail around Alliance, Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail around 1 inch in diameter reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying area of showers and thunderstorms spread strong non-thunderstorm wind gusts across Seward county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dept. of highways sensor near Seward measured a wind gust of 58 mph.  The winds were a heat burst-like event associated with a decaying area of showers and thunderstorms west of the region as temperatures rose to around 80 degrees while the dewpoint dropped 5 to 8 degrees.  The effects of the winds were a bit stronger west of the county where some scattered damage occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system and associated warm front over southeast Nebraska, accompanied by a weak upper level disturbance dropping southeast, help spawn a few early evening severe thunderstorms over southeast Nebraska.  The storms produced areas of strong and damaging winds over a small area of far southeast Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph blew a refrigerator from the yard into the side of a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures and ample moisture from southeast winds provided the ingredients necessary for isolated thunderstorms to produce hail across central and north central Nebraska during the evening hours.

A line of thunderstorms developed by late afternoon along a very weak nearly stationary front that stretched from southeast Nebraska into southwest Iowa.  The line of storms dropped slowly south and spun up several narrow non-supercell funnel clouds or tornadoes on the updraft portion of the leading edge.  An isolated storm also produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated by the public at around 60 mph downed a few tree limbs up to 5 feet long and a few inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system and associated warm front over southeast Nebraska, accompanied by a weak upper level disturbance dropping southeast, help spawn a few early evening severe thunderstorms over southeast Nebraska.  The storms produced areas of strong and damaging winds over a small area of far southeast Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at around 70 mph blew down a very large mature tree in town and also caused other tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system and associated warm front over southeast Nebraska, accompanied by a weak upper level disturbance dropping southeast, help spawn a few early evening severe thunderstorms over southeast Nebraska.  The storms produced areas of strong and damaging winds over a small area of far southeast Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph violently shook a spotter's vehicle west of Pawnee City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across eastern Colorado during the evening and moved across southern Nebraska overnight.  An isolated 3/4 hail report was received, in addition to locally heavy rainfall.

A small area of thunderstorms moved rapidly NNE across Red Willow county Nebraska during the late afternoon hours.  Although no severe weather was reported, a lightning strike injured a 9-year old boy in McCook.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 9-year old boy ran outside to retrieve a cardboard box before the storm arrived so it wouldn't blow away.  As he was retrieving the box which was near a yard light, lightning struck the light pole and jumped to a metal button on his coat. He complained of shoulder pain, a tingling sensation in his body and a metal taste in his mouth. He was taken to the hospital where tests were performed, then treated and released.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed over NW Kansas and SW Nebraska during the early evening hours. One long-lived supercell moved slowly east across Sheridan and Graham counties in Kansas, producing baseball-sized hail and winds of 70-80 mph. Several grain bins were damaged or destroyed, hundreds of homes suffered broken windows on the north side of the dwellings, and minor power outages occurred.  A grain bin was destroyed in the Danbury- Marion area in southern Red Willow county, Nebraska.  Some minor roof damage and downed tree limbs also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several irrigation systems were toppled, tree branches were snapped and shingles removed from homes across northern Dundy county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced some hail and strong winds over far western Sioux county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Also brief wind gusts estimated from 40 to near 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced hail, strong winds and some flash flooding over parts of the western Nebraska panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts from 64 to 79 mph recorded at the airport and near Scottsbluff, Nebraska.  Winds blew down some power poles and trees, and caused some roof damage to a few outbuildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced hail, strong winds and some flash flooding over parts of the western Nebraska panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused some flooding of basements in about 9 homes, as well as washed out a few county roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed with the heating of the day as a warm front lifted north across Nebraska. Previous days of rainfall resulted in an increased potential for flash flooding with a Flood Watch issued at 805 AM CST for portions of North Central and Southwest Nebraska. At 133 PM CST the Storms Prediction Center issued Tornado Watch number 384 that ran until 8 PM CST. Severe thunderstorms developed that led to numerous large hail reports in southwest and north central Nebraska. In addition, flash flooding occurred in Frontier county.

This was day 2 of a several day stretch of pulse-type severe thunderstorms that occurred across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa.  A cool northwesterly upper level wind flow prevailed during this stretch, with several embedded disturbances, while warm and unstable conditions were observed at the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was mostly pea-size, but a few were up to dime-size.  The hail covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A spring snowstorm hit northeast Nebraska from Thursday afternoon April 10th into the morning hours of April 11th.  A wet snowfall of 5 to 7 inches fell over sections of the region, accompanied by northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph.  Although the wetness of the snow prevented significant drifting, and much of it melted by the afternoon of the 11th, visibilities were reduced to a mile or less at times as the heavy wet snow was blown around by the strong winds.  Heavier snow totals included 7 inches at Neligh in Antelope county, 6.4 inches at Lorreto in Boone county, and around 6 inches south of Verdel and south of Wausa both in Knox county.||The heavy snow was the result of an unseasonably strong storm system that tracked from southwest Kansas across eastern Nebraska.  This system brought scattered thunderstorms into southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa Thursday the 10th as a warm front lifted north into southeast Nebraska and southern Iowa.  The system also spawned several tornadoes farther east across Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The coop observer at Neligh measured 7.0 inches of snow from this storm.

Severe thunderstorms developed over NW Kansas and SW Nebraska during the early evening hours. One long-lived supercell moved slowly east across Sheridan and Graham counties in Kansas, producing baseball-sized hail and winds of 70-80 mph. Several grain bins were damaged or destroyed, hundreds of homes suffered broken windows on the north side of the dwellings, and minor power outages occurred.  A grain bin was destroyed in the Danbury- Marion area in southern Red Willow county, Nebraska.  Some minor roof damage and downed tree limbs also occurred.

A winter storm covered south central Nebraska with a fresh blanket of 6 inches of snow on average. The snow rolled in around sunrise the morning of the 5th and continued into the evening hours. A wide swath of 5 to 8 inches of snow fell along U.S. Highway 6 and 34 from Arapahoe to Hastings, and then over toward Aurora and York. The National Weather Service Office north of Hastings measured 7.7 inches of new snow. Cambridge had 7.4 inches and Holdrege 7 inches.  There was some blowing and drifting reported.  Many, if not most, schools were closed at least one day due to the winter storm conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell in parts of Fillmore county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Parts of south central Nebraska were on the northern fringes of a winter storm and shared in accumulating snow as a result. A swath of 2 to 5 inches of snow fell along and north of a line from Lexington to Grand Island, and then to Columbus. The bands of heavier snowfall resulted in Lexington picking up 5 inches of snow, Kearney 3 inches and Central City 4 inches. Ord only reported about 2 inches of snow. Many area schools changed to alternative start and dismiss times to accommodate the snowy conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow was common across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very late winter snow event totaled anywhere from 1 to 5 inches across a small section of south central Nebraska. Accumulating snow was confined to just three counties: Valley, Sherman and Dawson.  Sherman and Valley counties reported 2 to 4 inches of snow.  In Dawson County, the accumulating snows were confined to areas north of Interstate 80 and west of State Highway 21. The area just north of Gothenburg near the Lincoln County line measured the most snow, which was around 5 inches.  Temperatures soared in the upper 50s the day after the snow, and all of the snow was melted within two days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of wet snow fell in parts of the county.

A thunderstorm complex that rolled across South Dakota during the morning and early afternoon hours of May 6th, pushed an outflow boundary into northern Nebraska.  Isolated severe storms developed on this boundary and tracked across far northeast Nebraska during the early to mid afternoon hours.  Additional storms would fire on the same boundary farther to the west and southwest later that afternoon.  Those storms eventually tracked across eastern Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The storm also produced 50 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed on an outflow boundary and ahead of a cold front over central and western Nebraska.  The storms were fed by moderately unstable air with surface temperatures in the 70s to around 80 and dewpoint temperatures in the 50s.  The storms pushed across eastern Nebraska and into southwest Iowa during the mid to late evening hours producing scattered areas of wind damage and some large hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph or more severely rocked a trailer near town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a cold front and moved southeast through the evening.  The storms produced large hail, strong wind gusts, heavy rain, and a brief tornado.

A narrow line of thunderstorms developed along a dry line over the western sections of northeast Nebraska during the mid afternoon hours of May 24th.  A few storms managed to produce wall clouds and some rotation.  However, warm air in the mid levels and marginal mid level lapse rates managed to squelch much of the activity a few hours after development. Before they dissipated or moved out of the region, one storm did manage to produce marginal severe size hail in far northeast Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was up to penny size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed on an outflow boundary and ahead of a cold front over central and western Nebraska.  The storms were fed by moderately unstable air with surface temperatures in the 70s to around 80 and dewpoint temperatures in the 50s.  The storms pushed across eastern Nebraska and into southwest Iowa during the mid to late evening hours producing scattered areas of wind damage and some large hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This storm also produced a possible funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over extreme northeast Nebraska along a warm front that extended east from low pressure in northeast Nebraska.  The thunderstorms produced isolated large hail reports in northeast Nebraska before lifting into South Dakota and northern Iowa where they further intensified producing more numerous hail reports along with a few tornadoes.  The whole system was associated with an unseasonably strong upper level system that had moved over western Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ground was covered with hail, some up to quarter size.

A large and unseasonably strong upper level low pressure system that was parked over the Rocky Mountains for several days fed moisture and instability northward across the plains north of a nearly stationary front that was positioned over southern Nebraska.  This resulted in a several days of heavy rain over portions of central and northeast Nebraska on May 22, 23 and 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several days of heavy rain caused the Loup River to flood from southeast of Genoa through southeast of Monroe.  The Loup River at Genoa, which is in Nance county just west of the Platte county line, reached a crest of 10.5 feet around 3am CDT on 5/25.  Flood stage is 9 feet.  The flooding closed a few county roads southwest through southeast of Monroe and also flooded farmland.

A moderate amount of instability developed across eastern Nebraska Sunday afternoon and evening September 28th as temperatures rose into the 80s and dewpoints reached the lower 60s.  As a cold front crossed the area Sunday evening, the instability aided in thunderstorm development, a few of which became severe.  The strongest storms produced mainly hail of an inch or less in diameter, but isolated storms produced strong winds.  The cold front was driven southeast by a fairly vigorous upper level trough that was dropping southeast out of Canada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the second severe thunderstorm that dropped hail at least 3/4 of an inch in diameter that night.

As an upper level low pressure system tracked along the United States and Canadian border, a weakening cold front pushed across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  Ahead of the front temperatures climbed into the upper 70s and dewpoint temperatures reached the lower to mid 60s.  The resultant instability and cold front helped to spawn severe thunderstorms across southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa, with hail up to 1 inch in diameter the primary severe parameter.

A low pressure area moved across Kansas into Nebraska and produced a band of heavy snow over extreme northeast Colorado and southwest Nebraska.  Yuma county Colorado received 6-10 inches of snow across the northern half of the county while Dundy county Nebraska picked up 6-10 inches across the northern 2/3 of the county.

An intense jet stream in the middle levels of the atmosphere mixed down to the surface with day time heating, resulting in a day of strong, gusty north winds across the region. All counties experienced wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph with several counties gusting near 60 mph.  No damage was reported.

A strong upper level low pressure system moved east across southern South Dakota on November 5th and 6th. Very strong northwest winds combined with up to 12 inches of snow to produce near whiteout conditions across Sheridan and Cherry Counties.  High winds of 35 to 45 with some gusts in excess of 60 mph occurred across the remainder of western and north central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds of 40 to 50 mph with higher gusts were common across Chase County.  The peak wind recorded at the Imperial ASOS was 59 mph at 1238 pm CST. High winds continued across Chase County into the afternoon hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system moved east across southern South Dakota on November 5th and 6th. Very strong northwest winds combined with up to 12 inches of snow to produce near whiteout conditions across Sheridan and Cherry Counties.  High winds of 35 to 45 with some gusts in excess of 60 mph occurred across the remainder of western and north central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow up to 6 inches deep fell across portions of western Cherry County.  Snow along with northwest winds of 40 to 50 mph broke off some trees and power poles and caused snow drifts up to 4 feet high.  Many rural homes lost electricity during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system moved east across southern South Dakota on November 5th and 6th. Very strong northwest winds combined with up to 12 inches of snow to produce near whiteout conditions across Sheridan and Cherry Counties.  High winds of 35 to 45 with some gusts in excess of 60 mph occurred across the remainder of western and north central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds of 40 to 50 mph with higher gusts combined with snow to cause near whiteout conditions.  Snow drifts up to 3 feet high closed rural roads.  More than 65 semi trucks were stranded in Valentine as conditions were too treacherous to travel.  Winds gusted to 59 mph at the Valentine ASOS at 1203 am CST November 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snowfall occurred across portions of extreme southwest Nebraska beginning in the late afternoon hours and continuing through the evening hours on November 10th. Chase and eastern Perkins County received storm total snowfall amounts ranging from 6 to 15 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of six inches was reported by a cooperative weather observer in Imperial at 8 pm CST with the storm total snowfall of 10.5 inches.  Up to 15 inches of snow was reported five miles southwest of Imperial.  Hourly snowfall rates of one and a half to two inches of snow per hour were observed at Imperial from 4 pm CST until the snowfall diminished at 1030 pm CST.  During this time, visibilities were occasionally reduced to a quarter mile or less.

An intense low pressure area developed over the Central Plains states.  Northwest winds of 45 to 70 mph developed in the strong surface pressure gradient west of the low pressure system which affected eastern Colorado, western Kansas and southwest Nebraska.  Several locations in northwest Kansas reported downed power lines and two vehicle rollovers were reported on Interstate 70 in Sherman county Kansas.
A strong pressure gradient produced high winds over parts of the nebraska panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several gusts around 60 mph were recorded near Hemingford and Alliance, Nebraska.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure across Montana into North Dakota and South Dakota provided for a prolonged snow event of nearly 48 hrs across North Central Nebraska. The event began during the early morning hours on January 20th and persisted until the late evening hours on January 21st. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 13 inches across Boyd and Holt counties in Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spencer hydroelectric plant reported storm total snowfall of 11 inches once the snow ended during the late evening hours of January 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved across the Central and Northern Plains December 13th and 14th, 2008.  Snowfall amounts of 2 to 5 inches occurred across Sheridan and Cherry County. The snow combined with strong northerly winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph to cause visibilities to fall to near zero, creating blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Valentine ASOS in eastern Cherry County, as well as a mesonet observation site reported visibility at a quarter mile or less from 1 am CST through 3am CST. Northerly winds of 25 to 35 mph with higher gusts persisted through mid afternoon on December 14th causing significant blowing and drifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A reinforcement of bitterly cold arctic air combined with northerly winds of 10 to 20 mph produced dangerous wind chills of 30 below to 40 below zero. These dangerous wind chills began during the evening of December 14th and continued through the morning of December 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very cold wind chill values of 30 below to 40 below zero were reached as northerly winds of 10 to 20 mph combined with arctic air.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving clipper system brought strong northwest winds to portions of north central Nebraska.  Northwest winds of 40 to 50 mph with peak wind gusts recorded around 60 mph occurred during the afternoon hours on December 30th, 2008.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 mph or greater occurred during the afternoon.  A peak wind gust of 61 mph occurred nearby at the Valentine ASOS site in northeast Cherry County at 120 pm CST with another peak wind gust of 60 mph at 229 pm CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid arctic air occurred across western and north central Nebraska on December 21st as a storm system swept southeast from the Dakotas into the lower Great Lakes region.  This resulted in dangerous wind chill values of 30 below zero to 40 below zero across much of western and north central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Air temperatures of 5 below to 10 below zero combined with northwest winds of 20 to 30 mph during the morning hours on December 21st.  This created dangerous wind chills as low as 37 degrees below zero in Rock County, taken from a mesonet site near Rose at 230 am CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp Arctic cold front combined with an upper level disturbance to produce a combination of dangerously cold wind chill values and snow and blowing snow.  Wind chill values dropped to 20 below to 25 below zero as visibilities occasionally fell to 1/4 mile or less.  Although total snowfall was mainly just an inch or two, the poor visibilities and locally slick roads caused several reports of multi-car accidents or multiple reports of vehicles in ditches.  Northwest winds sustained at 25 to 30 mph with gusts to 40 or 45 mph prevailed behind the front and temperatures fell into the lower to mid single digits.  This was just one day after readings climbed into the 40s and even some low 50s across much of the area.

Arctic high pressure that surged south across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa Tuesday evening February 19th, brought dangerously cold wind chill values to the region Wednesday morning February 20th.  Actual temperatures dropped to 5 below to 15 below zero with the coldest across northeast Nebraska and west central Iowa.  Sustained north winds of 12 to 20 mph (higher gusts were also observed) produced wind chill values that dropped into the 20 below to 30 below range over much of the region, with some values as cold as 35 below from Cedar and Wayne counties in northeast Nebraska southeast into Monona and Harrison counties of west central Iowa.||The extreme cold wind chills caused the cancellation of some schools across the region.

A powerful winter storm moved across the Central and Northern Plains December 13th and 14th, 2008.  Snowfall amounts of 2 to 5 inches occurred across Sheridan and Cherry County. The snow combined with strong northerly winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph to cause visibilities to fall to near zero, creating blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The nearby ASOS at Pine Ridge, South Dakota, reported visibility at a quarter mile or less from midnight MST through 3am MST. Northerly winds of 25 to 35 mph with higher gusts persisted through mid afternoon on December 14th causing significant blowing and drifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved across the Central and Northern Plains December 13th and 14th, 2008.  Snowfall amounts of 2 to 5 inches occurred across Sheridan and Cherry County. The snow combined with strong northerly winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph to cause visibilities to fall to near zero, creating blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The nearby ASOS at Pine Ridge, South Dakota, as well as a mesonet observation site in western Cherry County reported visibility at a quarter mile or less from midnight MST through 3am MST. Northerly winds of 25 to 35 mph with higher gusts persisted through mid afternoon on December 14th causing significant blowing and drifting.

Actual temperatures dropped to 5 below to 15 below zero with the coldest across northeast Nebraska and west central Iowa.  Sustained north winds of 12 to 20 mph (higher gusts were also observed) produced wind chill values that dropped into the 20 below to 30 below range over much of the region, with some values as cold as 35 below from Cedar and Wayne counties in northeast Nebraska southeast into Monona and Harrison counties of west central Iowa.||The extreme cold wind chills caused the cancellation of some schools across the region.

A fast moving upper level system moved from the southwestern United States across the plains tapping gulf moisture as it moved near eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  With Arctic air in place across the area...the moisture and forcing from the system produced freezing rain and sleet over southeast and east central Nebraska and southwest Iowa and heavy snow over northeast Nebraska and west central Iowa.  Freezing rain accumulations of 1/4 to 1/3 inch were common from far southeast Nebraska into far southwest Iowa, with 1/2 to 3/4 inch sleet mixed with freezing rain accumulations common just north of there including the Lincoln and Omaha areas.  Farther north and northwest the precipitation was mostly snow...although some sleet also fell...with 6 to 11 inches of snow reported along and northeast of a Dodge to Norfolk to Neligh line.  Some of the heavier snow totals included around 11 inches at Walthill and near Battle Creek and around 10 inches near Norfolk and Wayne.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a relatively brief period of freezing rain or freezing drizzle, heavy snow spread across Platte county during the evening hours of 12/18.  Snowfall over the county ranged from 3 to 6 inches across the south to 6 to 8 inches over the north. The snow caused the cancellation of some area schools.

Strong northwest winds developed behind a cold front on November 14th.  Numerous locations in western Nebraska reported sustained winds of 40 mph with gusts as high as 64 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 mph with higher gusts were common across Garden County.  The peak wind gust of 55 mph was recorded on a Nebraska Department of Roads mesonet site at Lisco at 206 pm CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of strong jet stream winds aloft, moisture streaming into the Central Plains from the Gulf of Mexico, and a large scale upper level low pressure system spinning over Arizona, brought a swath of heavy snow to parts of Southwest and West Central Nebraska. The snow began on the evening of March 16th and continued through March 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain occurred briefly before the snow in Chase County. Heavy snow ranging from 4 to 8 inches occurred across Chase County. The highest report of 8 inches was reported 8 miles west of Champion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system moved from Wyoming into South Dakota bringing heavy snow to portions of North Central Nebraska during the night of March 30th through the morning of March 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow occurred across western Cherry County during the night of March 30th into the morning of March 31st.  Snowfall amounts ranging from 4 to 10 inches occurred with the highest amount of 10 inches reported 5 miles west northwest of Cody.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow totaling 8 inches was reported by the public three miles south of Grant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system that helped spawn killer tornadoes over the Tennessee Valley brought areas of heavy snow and blowing snow to southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  The system tracked from the Texas panhandle to the lower great lakes region and pulled up 50 and 60 degree surface dew points into southeast Missouri and southern Illinois.||Snow from this system began around sunrise Tuesday morning February 5th and lasted until mid morning on the 6th.  Six to over 8 inches fell across much of Richardson and Nemaha counties, and north winds that frequently gusted over 25 mph caused significant blowing and drifting snow.  Many schools across the area were closed on the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavier snow amounts included 8.5 inches in Rulo, 7.5 inches at Salem, 7 inches in Shubert and 6 inches at Falls City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moving out of the Southern Rockies brought a significant springtime winter storm to western and north central Nebraska on April 10th and 11th. Heavy snowfall with blowing snow reduced visibilities to as low as a quarter mile for several hours as north winds of 20 to 30 mph with occasional gusts to 40 mph occurred. Snowfall amounts ranging from 4 to 13 inches fell with the heaviest amounts occurring across the central Sandhills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall with amounts ranging from 10 to 12 inches occurred across  Thomas county. North winds of 20 to 35 mph caused considerable blowing and drifting snow with the Thedford AWOS reporting visibility reduced to a 1/4 to 1/2 mile for 8 hours with hazardous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed over NW Kansas and SW Nebraska during the early evening hours. One long-lived supercell moved slowly east across Sheridan and Graham counties in Kansas, producing baseball-sized hail and winds of 70-80 mph. Several grain bins were damaged or destroyed, hundreds of homes suffered broken windows on the north side of the dwellings, and minor power outages occurred.  A grain bin was destroyed in the Danbury- Marion area in southern Red Willow county, Nebraska.  Some minor roof damage and downed tree limbs also occurred.

A spring storm system brought heavy snow to Boyd and Holt county in the eastern part of north central Nebraska. Snowfall amounts ranged from six to ten inches in Boyd county, and four to nine inches in Holt county. Northwest winds of 15 to 30 mph caused visibility to be reduced to a quarter mile for up to two hours with blowing and drifting snow and hazardous driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow occurred across Boyd county on April 25th, with amounts ranging from 6 to 10 inches. A Cooperative observer 1 mile east of Gross, reported 10 inches of snowfall. Northwest winds of 15 to 30 mph also caused visibility to be reduced to a quarter mile for up to two hours, with blowing and drifting snow and hazardous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A spring snowstorm hit northeast Nebraska from Thursday afternoon April 10th into the morning hours of April 11th.  A wet snowfall of 5 to 7 inches fell over sections of the region, accompanied by northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph.  Although the wetness of the snow prevented significant drifting, and much of it melted by the afternoon of the 11th, visibilities were reduced to a mile or less at times as the heavy wet snow was blown around by the strong winds.  Heavier snow totals included 7 inches at Neligh in Antelope county, 6.4 inches at Lorreto in Boone county, and around 6 inches south of Verdel and south of Wausa both in Knox county.||The heavy snow was the result of an unseasonably strong storm system that tracked from southwest Kansas across eastern Nebraska.  This system brought scattered thunderstorms into southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa Thursday the 10th as a warm front lifted north into southeast Nebraska and southern Iowa.  The system also spawned several tornadoes farther east across Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The coop observer at Loretto measured 6.4 inches of snow from this storm with some drifts up to 3 feet.  Heavy snow and blowing snow caused near zero visibilities at times on east-west roads in the county.  Power outages were reported in rural areas in the county's northern half due to the wet snow and the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms, including a few supercells, developed along a cold front that was pushing into eastern Nebraska and also along a weak warm front that extended just ahead of the cold front.  The storms were fed by a very strong low level jet that increased in intensity and helped create the wind shear favorable for the supercells, including one which produced a short-lived tornado in Johnson, Nebraska.  The storms also produced a small area of very heavy rain.  Virginia in Gage county reported around 4 inches with a little over 2 inches reported in Tecumseh which is in Johnson county.  The heavy rain produced isolated flash flooding and river flooding.||This weather system eventually produced 3 to 8 inches or more of heavy wet snow on April 25th from northern Nebraska into North Dakota and western Minnesota.  Freezing rain was also reported over sections of eastern South Dakota from this storm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down on the southwest edge of Johnson near Chestnut and 1st Streets.  The tornado moved northeast across town causing EF1 damage to several buildings in downtown Johnson along Main Street between 2nd and 3rd Streets.  The tornado weakened as it approached the high school and then lifted around Walnut and 5th Streets.||Roofing material and the facade of several buildings were peeled or collapsed.  Hardwood and softwood trees were snapped along the path and 2 garages were pushed off of their foundations.  A building next to the fire station also had a large garage door blown in.  No injuries were reported.

Severe thunderstorms developed with the heating of the day as a warm front lifted north across Nebraska. Previous days of rainfall resulted in an increased potential for flash flooding with a Flood Watch issued at 805 AM CST for portions of North Central and Southwest Nebraska. At 133 PM CST the Storms Prediction Center issued Tornado Watch number 384 that ran until 8 PM CST. Severe thunderstorms developed that led to numerous large hail reports in southwest and north central Nebraska. In addition, flash flooding occurred in Frontier county.
A few severe thunderstorms moved over parts of the Nebraska panhandle and produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail from 1.0 to 2.5 inches in diameter fell along track.

A potent early spring winter storm impacted the area on April 10th as a large upper level storm system moved from the southwestern U.S. through Kansas and into Iowa.  This system pulled in plenty of moisture, and enough warm air, that the event started out as all rain.  Colder air built in across northwestern areas as this system moved to the east, changing the precipitation over to snow.  Across Dawson and Valley counties, 6 to 7 inches of snow was reported.  Further southeast, including areas into Phelps, Buffalo, and Howard counties, 2 to 5 inches was reported.  There were not significant impacts from this storm, but some area schools did postpone or cancel classes and other events.

Several ice jams from around West Point in Cuming county downstream to near Hooper and Winslow caused flooding of a few county roads and agricultural lowlands along the river.  The flooding also closed Highway 275 near Crowell for a while.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Elkhorn River at West Point fluctuated 1 or 2 feet above and below its 12 foot flood stage from around midnight CST on March 1st until around 6 pm CDT on March 9th.  The crest during this time period was 13.3 feet at 8 pm CST on March 4th.  The flood waters closed Highway 275 near West Point for a few days and also flooded a few county roads and agricultural lowlands in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense upper level low pressure system tracked across western Nebraska on May 1st, bringing snowfall amounts up to 4 inches and near blizzard conditions to western Nebraska.  Northwest winds of 40 to 50 mph with a few gusts to around 60 mph reduced visibility to less than a quarter mile for several hours during the morning and afternoon hours causing the closure of Interstate 80 west of Ogallala.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Nebraska Department of Roads mesonet site reported northwest winds of 48 mph with gusts to 66 mph at 608 am MST.  Strong northwest winds of 40 to 50 mph and higher gusts continued into the early afternoon hours with snow accumulations up to 4 inches causing blowing and drifting snow and near blizzard conditions.

Northwest winds of 40 to 50 mph with a few gusts to around 60 mph reduced visibility to less than a quarter mile for several hours during the morning and afternoon hours causing the closure of Interstate 80 west of Ogallala.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds of 40 to 50 mph and higher gusts occurred during the morning and continued into the early afternoon hours with snow accumulations of 4 inches causing considerable blowing and drifting snow and near blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center that stalled over northeast Colorado and the associated warm front across northern Kansas resulted in showers and thunderstorms into southwest Nebraska May 23, 2008. The persistent southeast surface wind flow provided deep moisture across southwest Nebraska that fueled training thunderstorms resulting in increased runoff from saturated ground and flash flooding. In addition, thunderstorms developing in an unstable atmosphere produced isolated large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding to roads on the west side of Chappell and to roads near the fairgrounds. Lodgepole Creek flowed out of its banks and caused lowland flooding.

A thunderstorm complex developed along a dry line/weak cool front just southwest of the region during the late afternoon hours of May 25th.  The storms spread quickly northeast along the boundary through mid evening, causing areas of wind damage from southwest of Lincoln through the south part of Omaha and then into southwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds measured at 74 mph by weather equipment caused areas of tree damage around the Fairbury area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal surface trough followed by a cold front touched off a few severe thunderstorms across eastern Nebraska.
A pre-frontal surface trough followed by a cold front touched off a few severe thunderstorms across eastern Nebraska.

An increasing low level jet brought moist and unstable air into eastern Nebraska during the early morning hours of July 5th.  Slow moving severe thunderstorms developed as a result an hour or so before sunsrise.  The activity gradually weakened after mid morning as warmer air aloft spread over the region capping the atmosphere and shutting down the precipitation.  Besides producing pockets of large hail over the area, localized rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches were reported in a few hours time which caused isolated flash flooding and river flooding.

A warm front lifted north across the region during the afternoon and evening of May 29th followed by a weak cool front passage later that night.  As the warm front passed, temperatures warmed into the lower to mid 80s and dewpoint temperatures climbed into the 65 to 70 degree range.  The whole system was aided by an intense upper level low pressure area that moved from the eastern Rockies into the northern plains.  The combination of the high instability in place, the 2 fronts and the upper level disturbance created conditions that were favorable not only for severe thunderstorms, a few of which were supercells that produced tornadoes, but also areas of very heavy rainfall that produced flash flooding that night and river and stream flooding that lasted a day or two later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thundestorm winds estimated at 60 mph flipped a semi trailer on Interstate 80 southeast of Greenwood.

A weak cold front touched off thunderstorms in northeast Nebraska during the early afternoon hours of July 17th.  The storms produced a few large hail and strong wind reports as they moved southeast.  The storms worked ahead of the front from late afternoon into the early evening hours as they encountered an even more moist environment.  The storms movement slowed down and tracked across the same area for several hours producing some reports of very heavy rain and flash flooding south of Lincoln.  In some areas the rain fell on already wet ground with rivers running high from heavy rain the previous day...thus some river flooding developed or was prolonged.  In addition...other storms redeveloped near the weak front in northeast Nebraska by early evening producing a few additional reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed in the Pender area by thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An increasing low level jet brought moist and unstable air into eastern Nebraska during the early morning hours of July 5th.  Slow moving severe thunderstorms developed as a result an hour or so before sunsrise.  The activity gradually weakened after mid morning as warmer air aloft spread over the region capping the atmosphere and shutting down the precipitation.  Besides producing pockets of large hail over the area, localized rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches were reported in a few hours time which caused isolated flash flooding and river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to nickel-size was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow winds tore a roof off a hay shed, destroyed and damaged grain bins, broke off four power poles, uprooted trees, and tore rain gutters off a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across the region during the afternoon and evening of May 29th followed by a weak cool front passage later that night.  As the warm front passed, temperatures warmed into the lower to mid 80s and dewpoint temperatures climbed into the 65 to 70 degree range.  The whole system was aided by an intense upper level low pressure area that moved from the eastern Rockies into the northern plains.  The combination of the high instability in place, the 2 fronts and the upper level disturbance created conditions that were favorable not only for severe thunderstorms, a few of which were supercells that produced tornadoes, but also areas of very heavy rainfall that produced flash flooding that night and river and stream flooding that lasted a day or two later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph overturned 9 center pivots near Monroe and also caused scattered tree damage in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly a week of significant rains culminated with a heavy rain event on May 29th and produced significant flooding along Shell Creek from the Newman Grove area downstream eventually reaching the Schuyler area.  Shell Creek northeast of Columbus reached just over 22 feet around 1pm CDT on May 30th, flood stage is 20 feet.  The creek had a 6 or 7 hour flat crest centered on its peak of 22.1 feet.  The gage northeast of Columbus was above flood stage from around 7am CDT on the 30th through 330pm on the 31st.||The flooding in Madison county closed a few county roads near Newman Grove and also briefly closed Highway 91 near Lindsay.  Flooding along Shell Creek was more significant downstream in Platte and Colfax counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across the region during the afternoon and evening of May 29th followed by a weak cool front passage later that night. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall during the evening of May 29th culminated nearly a week of scattered heavy rain producing thunderstorms that occurred along the Missouri River basin.  The heavy rains caused the Missouri River to flood from near its confluence with the Platte River near Plattsmouth downstream through Rulo which is in extreme southeast Richardson county.  The Flooding from this event along the Missouri River in Richardson county began during the mid afternoon hours of May 30th.  Additional heavy rains during the first week of June kept the river above flood stage in Nemaha county until the 3rd week of June.  The river at Rulo crested just above 25 feet early in the evening of June 14th, flood stage is 17 feet.  The flooding affected agricultural lowlands and also flooded a few county roads in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and moist air worked its way into eastern Nebraska during the early morning hours of June 15th.  As a cold front pushed across the area a little after sunrise, severe thunderstorms started developing from north of Columbus south and southeast toward Lincoln and Omaha.  The thunderstorm complex then pushed across southeast Nebraska with the severe weather ending across the area before noon that day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarter size fell for several minutes in the Wilber area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center that stalled over northeast Colorado and the associated warm front across northern Kansas resulted in showers and thunderstorms into southwest Nebraska May 23, 2008. The persistent southeast surface wind flow provided deep moisture across southwest Nebraska that fueled training thunderstorms resulting in increased runoff from saturated ground and flash flooding. In addition, thunderstorms developing in an unstable atmosphere produced isolated large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lincoln County Emergency Manager reported several county roads due to heavy rain and flash flooding. The roads closures included Effenbeck Road, the Brady-Moorefield Road, Fox Creek Road, and Cut Creek Road in east central and southeast Lincoln County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed with the heating of the day as a warm front lifted north across Nebraska. Previous days of rainfall resulted in an increased potential for flash flooding with a Flood Watch issued at 805 AM CST for portions of North Central and Southwest Nebraska. At 133 PM CST the Storms Prediction Center issued Tornado Watch number 384 that ran until 8 PM CST. Severe thunderstorms developed that led to numerous large hail reports in southwest and north central Nebraska. In addition, flash flooding occurred in Frontier county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall during the evening of May 29th culminated nearly a week of scattered heavy rain producing thunderstorms that occurred along the Platte River basin.  The heavy rains caused the Platte River to flood from just northeast of Ashland downstream through the Louisville area then to its mouth with the Missouri River near Plattsmouth.  The Platte River reached a crest of 21.1 at Ashland around 9am CDT on May 31st, flood stage is 20 feet.  The river crested at 10.6 feet at Louisville around 1pm on May 31st, flood stage is 9 feet.  The river remained above flood stage at both points into June 1st.  The crest at Louisville was among the highest on record there.  The flooding closed a few county roads in the area and also flooded farmland.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms early on June 24th caused the Big Blue River to flood in Saline county beginning around noon CDT on 6/24.  Additional heavy rain producing thunderstorms later that afternoon added to the flooding and kept the Blue River around the Crete area above flood stage until just before midnight on the 26th.  The river at Crete crested at 19.5 feet for about 24 hours centered near midnight on the 25th.  Flood stage is 18 feet.  The flooding affected mainly agricultural lowlands, but also closed a few county roads in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across the region during the afternoon and evening of May 29th followed by a weak cool front passage later that night.  As the warm front passed, temperatures warmed into the lower to mid 80s and dewpoint temperatures climbed into the 65 to 70 degree range.  The whole system was aided by an intense upper level low pressure area that moved from the eastern Rockies into the northern plains.  The combination of the high instability in place, the 2 fronts and the upper level disturbance created conditions that were favorable not only for severe thunderstorms, a few of which were supercells that produced tornadoes, but also areas of very heavy rainfall that produced flash flooding that night and river and stream flooding that lasted a day or two later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Non-thunderstorm south winds were measured at 62 mph by Awos at the Plattsmouth airport.  These winds occurred northeast of a thunderstorm complex that produced severe weather to the southwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal surface trough followed by a cold front touched off a few severe thunderstorms across eastern Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to golfball-size covered the ground from along the Madison county line north of Lindsay into the Lindsay area.  The hail was driven by winds estimated at around 55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal surface trough followed by a cold front touched off a few severe thunderstorms across eastern Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarter-size was reported just southwest through southeast of Ft. Calhoun.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal surface trough followed by a cold front touched off a few severe thunderstorms across eastern Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to nickel-size was reported from about 6 miles south southeast of Ft. Calhoun into the northern part of Omaha near the junction of Highway 75 and Interstate 680.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal surface trough followed by a cold front touched off a few severe thunderstorms across eastern Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to penny-size was reported near Interstate 80 and 13th Street.  This was from another storm that developed just to the southeast of the first one that produced large hail from the Ft. Calhoun area into north Omaha.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across the region during the afternoon and evening of May 29th followed by a weak cool front passage later that night.  As the warm front passed, temperatures warmed into the lower to mid 80s and dewpoint temperatures climbed into the 65 to 70 degree range.  The whole system was aided by an intense upper level low pressure area that moved from the eastern Rockies into the northern plains.  The combination of the high instability in place, the 2 fronts and the upper level disturbance created conditions that were favorable not only for severe thunderstorms, a few of which were supercells that produced tornadoes, but also areas of very heavy rainfall that produced flash flooding that night and river and stream flooding that lasted a day or two later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall during the evening of May 29th culminated nearly a week of scattered heavy rain producing thunderstorms that occurred along the Missouri River basin.  The heavy rains caused the Missouri River to flood from near its confluence with the Platte River near Plattsmouth downstream through Rulo which is in extreme southeast Richardson county.  The Flooding from this event along the Missouri River in Nemaha county began during the mid afternoon hours of May 30th.  Additional heavy rains during the first week of June kept the river above flood stage in Nemaha county until the 3rd week of June.  The river at Brownville crested at around 40 feet late in the evening of June 13th, flood stage is 32 feet.  The flooding affected agricultural lowlands and also flooded a few county roads in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front and upper level disturbance encountered rich low level moisture, resulting in the development of supercell thunderstorms across portions of southwest into north central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five center pivot irrigation systems were overturned from thunderstorm wind gusts south and southeast of Bartlett.  In addition, a grain bin was pushed in and wind mills were destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed with the heating of the day as a warm front lifted north across Nebraska. Previous days of rainfall resulted in an increased potential for flash flooding with a Flood Watch issued at 805 AM CST for portions of North Central and Southwest Nebraska. At 133 PM CST the Storms Prediction Center issued Tornado Watch number 384 that ran until 8 PM CST. Severe thunderstorms developed that led to numerous large hail reports in southwest and north central Nebraska. In addition, flash flooding occurred in Frontier county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser reported a brief tornado touchdown over open rangeland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An increasing low level jet brought moist and unstable air into eastern Nebraska during the early morning hours of July 5th.  Slow moving severe thunderstorms developed as a result an hour or so before sunsrise.  The activity gradually weakened after mid morning as warmer air aloft spread over the region capping the atmosphere and shutting down the precipitation.  Besides producing pockets of large hail over the area, localized rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches were reported in a few hours time which caused isolated flash flooding and river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2 to over 4 inches was reported in the Fairbury area from slow moving thunderstorms that repeatedly moved across the area for a several hour period.  The heavy rain caused flash flooding near the Crystal Springs Campground prompting some campers to be moved.  The flash flooding also caused a county road to be closed briefly just north of Fairbury.

The heavy rain caused considerable field flooding with some of the water flowing over county roads in the area, possibly from Silver Creek.  The water also flooded across Highway 79 between County Roads J and K for a while.

An approaching cold front and upper level disturbance encountered rich low level moisture, resulting in the development of supercell thunderstorms across portions of southwest into north central Nebraska.

A large upper level trough located over the western U.S. was ejecting pieces of energy out over the Central Plains.  Ahead of this trough at the surface, low pressure developed over eastern Colorado, helping to push a warm front north into the region.  Storms first initiated over western Kansas along the dry line in the early afternoon hours, with more development later on along the warm front in the evening.  Several strong to severe thunderstorms moved into central Nebraska during the late afternoon hours and these spread east during the evening.  This event was also the first in a series of nights in which many areas saw heavy rain.

Large upper level trough over the western U.S. continued to eject energy out over the Central Plains, with a surface low remaining anchored over eastern Colorado with a stationary boundary draped east through the region.  Storms once again initially developed during the early afternoon near the dry line over western Kansas and drifted east; with the increase of the low level jet helping storms which had developed along the stationary front continue on well into the late evening hours.  It was another night of efficient storms in regard to rainfall, dropping several inches over portions of south-central Nebraska, causing additional flooding problems.

A strong cold front passing slowly southeast through the Central Plains provided a focus for strong to severe thunderstorm development. Initial thunderstorms formed in the early afternoon along this front across portions of northern and western Nebraska, as well as western Kansas, and developed into a bow echo as it moved southeast.  The storms moved into central Nebraska during the late afternoon hours and produced hail up to one inch in diameter and winds up to 60 mph. The storms exited the region before midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front touched off thunderstorms in northeast Nebraska during the early afternoon hours of July 17th.  The storms produced a few large hail and strong wind reports as they moved southeast.  The storms worked ahead of the front from late afternoon into the early evening hours as they encountered an even more moist environment.  The storms movement slowed down and tracked across the same area for several hours producing some reports of very heavy rain and flash flooding south of Lincoln.  In some areas the rain fell on already wet ground with rivers running high from heavy rain the previous day...thus some river flooding developed or was prolonged.  In addition...other storms redeveloped near the weak front in northeast Nebraska by early evening producing a few additional reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager of Saunders county was nearly blown off the road by thunderstorm winds estimated at 70 mph.  Two wheels of his vehicle were briefly lifted off the road.

An upper level low pressure system and accompanying surface low crossing the Northern Plains pushed a cold front through a good portion of south-central Nebraska by mid afternoon on the 25th.  Decent moisture, instability, and shear aided in the development of strong to severe thunderstorms along and ahead of this cold front which was draped along the Kansas/Nebraska border by evening.  Penny to half dollar size hail was reported with some of these storms, along with strong winds near 60 mph.

Decent moisture, instability, and shear aided in the development of strong to severe thunderstorms along and ahead of this cold front which was draped along the Kansas/Nebraska border by evening.  Penny to half dollar size hail was reported with some of these storms, along with strong winds near 60 mph.

An upper level low pressure system and accompanying surface low crossing the Northern Plains pushed a cold front through a good portion of south-central Nebraska by mid afternoon on the 25th.  Decent moisture, instability, and shear aided in the development of strong to severe thunderstorms along and ahead of this cold front which was draped along the Kansas/Nebraska border by evening.  Penny to half dollar size hail was reported with some of these storms, along with strong winds near 60 mph.

An area of thunderstorms, including a few supercells, developed along a cold front that was pushing into eastern Nebraska and also along a weak warm front that extended just ahead of the cold front.  The storms were fed by a very strong low level jet that increased in intensity and helped create the wind shear favorable for the supercells, including one which produced a short-lived tornado in Johnson, Nebraska.  The storms also produced a small area of very heavy rain.  Virginia in Gage county reported around 4 inches with a little over 2 inches reported in Tecumseh which is in Johnson county.  The heavy rain produced isolated flash flooding and river flooding.||This weather system eventually produced 3 to 8 inches or more of heavy wet snow on April 25th from northern Nebraska into North Dakota and western Minnesota.  Freezing rain was also reported over sections of eastern South Dakota from this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2 to 6 inches in a few hours time caused flooding along Sampson Creek and Turkey Creek which washed out 3 bridges and caused county road damage southwest of Tecumseh. Damage in Johnson county to roads and bridges was estimated at 1 million dollars.  The flooding continued into Pawnee county.  The flood waters also affected agricultural lowlands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed over NW Kansas and SW Nebraska during the early evening hours. One long-lived supercell moved slowly east across Sheridan and Graham counties in Kansas, producing baseball-sized hail and winds of 70-80 mph. Several grain bins were damaged or destroyed, hundreds of homes suffered broken windows on the north side of the dwellings, and minor power outages occurred.  A grain bin was destroyed in the Danbury- Marion area in southern Red Willow county, Nebraska.  Some minor roof damage and downed tree limbs also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Marion, a grain bin was blown over in the SW corner of town, a garage was damaged and several power poles were down. In Danbury, a tree was blown onto a porch, a pivot irrigation system was toppled and tree and mobile home damage was reported west of town.

Large upper level trough over the western U.S. continued to eject energy out over the Central Plains, with a surface low remaining anchored over eastern Colorado with a stationary boundary draped east through the region.  Storms once again initially developed during the early afternoon near the dry line over western Kansas and drifted east; with the increase of the low level jet helping storms which had developed along the stationary front continue on well into the late evening hours.  It was another night of efficient storms in regard to rainfall, dropping several inches over portions of south-central Nebraska, causing additional flooding problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With six and three quarters of an inch of rain over a 48 hour period, the Crooked Creek overflowed its banks by two to three feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dumped just over two and three-quarters of an inch of rain during the early morning hours. This brought the 24 hour total to nearly 4 inches. This caused some flooding of many roads across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous areas in Franklin County picked up 3 to 4 inches of rain. This caused many rural roads to flood.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dropped 3 to 5 inches in this area. Several rural roads were flooded by this heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across the region during the afternoon and evening of May 29th followed by a weak cool front passage later that night.  As the warm front passed, temperatures warmed into the lower to mid 80s and dewpoint temperatures climbed into the 65 to 70 degree range.  The whole system was aided by an intense upper level low pressure area that moved from the eastern Rockies into the northern plains.  The combination of the high instability in place, the 2 fronts and the upper level disturbance created conditions that were favorable not only for severe thunderstorms, a few of which were supercells that produced tornadoes, but also areas of very heavy rainfall that produced flash flooding that night and river and stream flooding that lasted a day or two later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 to 70 mph blew over a large propane tank near Boone and damaged farm outbuildings at several farmsteads.  Large tree limbs were also downed in the area which blocked Highway 39 for a while.  At least 6 center pivots were flipped in the general area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed north of a stalled frontal boundary and low pressure system that extended from north central Kansas into southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 65 mph near the intersection of Highway 75 and Business Route 75 knocked down 5 power poles and 2 stop signs.  This prompted the closure of Business 75 until the power poles were repaired.  Trees were also toppled or damaged in the area as were farm outbuildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across the region during the afternoon and evening of May 29th followed by a weak cool front passage later that night.  As the warm front passed, temperatures warmed into the lower to mid 80s and dewpoint temperatures climbed into the 65 to 70 degree range.  The whole system was aided by an intense upper level low pressure area that moved from the eastern Rockies into the northern plains.  The combination of the high instability in place, the 2 fronts and the upper level disturbance created conditions that were favorable not only for severe thunderstorms, a few of which were supercells that produced tornadoes, but also areas of very heavy rainfall that produced flash flooding that night and river and stream flooding that lasted a day or two later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was a continuation of a long-tracked tornado that started in Jewel county Kansas and tracked into Thayer county Nebraska before crossing into Jefferson county Nebraska.  The tornado did EF3 damage in Kansas and EF2 damage in Jefferson county.  ||In Jefferson county the tornado started 2 miles west and 1 1/4 mile north of Reynolds, knocking over large tree branches, snapping trees and overturning several center pivot irrigation systems.  The storm tracked northeast from there continuing to snap wooden power poles and causing additional tree damage as well as farm outbuilding damage.  The tornado crossed the Little Blue River 2 3/4 miles northwest of Fairbury before turning east where it attained EF2 strength.  There it tore the roof off of a house and destroyed a garage and shed 1 mile west of Highway 15.  Heading east from there it hit the Fairbury airport where hangers were damaged.  East of the airport, a large barn was destroyed and large trees snapped.  The tornado finally lifted about 3 miles east and 3 miles north of Fairbury.  From the there the parent thunderstorm continued to produce additional wind damage from eastern Jefferson county into Gage county.  In total the tornado caused serious damage to 5 houses in Jefferson county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management estimated thunderstorm wind gusts of around 60 mph between Cedar Rapids and Saint Edwards.  The strong winds caused scattered tree damage in the Cedar Rapids area.
