A strong low pressure system over central Kansas combined with a strong high over Montana to produce an extremely tight surface pressure gradient over the Tri-State region.  Northwest winds gusting 45 to 55 mph were common during the day with a number of reports near 60 mph.
A strong cold front and tight surface pressure gradient created high winds across north central Nebraska on October 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusted to 64 mph at 1223 pm MST at the Nebraska Department of Roads weather station in eastern Sheridan County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Evening thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Nebraska as a surface front passed through the Central Plains.  A few of these thunderstorms were on the strong to severe side, with penny size hail reported in York County, and quarter size hail reported in Sherman.

An upper level shortwave trough crossing through the High Plains aided in the development of scattered thunderstorms across western portions of south central Nebraska.  One thunderstorm became strong enough to drop nickel size hail in Dawson County.

A surface frontal boundary stalled across the Central Plains provided a focus for late evening thunderstorm development across eastern portions of south central Nebraska.  The most significant activity occurred in Thayer County, where quarter size hail and over 3 inches of rain fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water ran out of area fields and across county road C. Over 3 inches of rain fell in the southeastern corner of Thayer county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Evening thunderstorms developed across western portions of south central Nebraska as a weak upper level disturbance crossed the Central Plains.  A few of these thunderstorms become strong enough to drop hail, with penny to nickel size hail reported in Furnas County, and quarter size hail in Valley County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel size hail reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure area over Nebraska produced snow and very strong winds over much of the Nebraska panhandle resulting in some road closures due to very low visibilities and some accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snowfall combined with strong winds to produce significant blowing snow.  Interstate 80 was closed for a time due to many accidents from significant icing on the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure area over Nebraska produced snow and very strong winds over much of the Nebraska panhandle resulting in some road closures due to very low visibilities and some accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts estimated from one to two inches combined with wind gusts up to 65 mph to produce significant blowing snow.  Power was knocked out for over a day at the Alliance airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Canadian air mass plunged southeast into the Central Plains during the late morning and afternoon hours of October 26th as temperatures in the 30s and 40s replaced 60s to around 70 which were observed the previous afternoon.  The cold air was ushered into the region on northwest winds that were sustained at 30 to 35 mph with gusts of 45 to 55 mph.  A few wind gusts reached high wind criteria of 58 mph or greater for a brief period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind of 59 mph was measured by the Omaha-Eppley Asos at 236pm CDT.  The winds were the result of Canadian air spilling southeast across the plains and caused scattered tree damage in town.  Around 4,000 OPPD customers also briefly lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Canadian air mass plunged southeast into the Central Plains during the late morning and afternoon hours of October 26th as temperatures in the 30s and 40s replaced 60s to around 70 which were observed the previous afternoon.  The cold air was ushered into the region on northwest winds that were sustained at 30 to 35 mph with gusts of 45 to 55 mph.  A few wind gusts reached high wind criteria of 58 mph or greater for a brief period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusts estimated at close to 60 mph caused an old vacant building to collapse in Union.  The winds were the result of Canadian air spilling southeast across the plains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along an area of low pressure that extended northeast across southwest Nebraska by late afternoon. The thunderstorms initially produced a landspout tornado and large hail, but then transitioned into damaging winds as they moved eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The winds brought down large tree branches throughout North Platte.  The frame of a new pharmacy being built near the hospital collapsed in the wind.  Corn crops were also damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: July 16, 2008 a surface boundary across the northwest became the focus for late afternoon severe thunderstorm development. Severe thunderstorms produced several reports of large hail with up to 3 inches in diameter falling in south central Cherry county.
July 24, 2008, a semi-stationary front extended southeast across western Nebraska. Moist, unstable air flowed north for favorable severe thunderstorm development east of the panhandle.

A cold front that crossed eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the evening hours of August 27th caused a short line of thunderstorms to develop north of Omaha.  The storms produced a few marginally severe size hail reports.

Afternoon thunderstorms developed along a surface boundary draped across south central Nebraska as an upper level disturbance moved across the plains.  These storms continued into the late evening hours, with winds to 60 mph, hail up to the size of quarters, and locally heavy rain of up to 7 inches.

Afternoon thunderstorms developed along a surface boundary draped across south central Nebraska as an upper level disturbance moved across the plains.  These storms continued into the late evening hours, with winds to 60 mph, hail up to the size of quarters, and locally heavy rain of up to 7 inches.

As an upper level low pressure system tracked along the United States and Canadian border, a weakening cold front pushed across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  Ahead of the front temperatures climbed into the upper 70s and dewpoint temperatures reached the lower to mid 60s.  The resultant instability and cold front helped to spawn severe thunderstorms across southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa, with hail up to 1 inch in diameter the primary severe parameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarter-size fell for a little over 10 minutes in the Auburn area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm and moist air worked its way into eastern Nebraska during the early morning hours of June 15th.  As a cold front pushed across the area a little after sunrise, severe thunderstorms started developing from north of Columbus south and southeast toward Lincoln and Omaha.  The thunderstorm complex then pushed across southeast Nebraska with the severe weather ending across the area before noon that day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported hail up to baseball-size fell in the Milford area.  The hail caused extensive vehicle damage, especially at a local car dealership and a local business where over 140 vehicles were damaged.  Some houses also sustained roof and siding damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center that stalled over northeast Colorado and the associated warm front across northern Kansas resulted in showers and thunderstorms into southwest Nebraska May 23, 2008. The persistent southeast surface wind flow provided deep moisture across southwest Nebraska that fueled training thunderstorms resulting in increased runoff from saturated ground and flash flooding. In addition, thunderstorms developing in an unstable atmosphere produced isolated large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow winds bent a fiberglass light pole on the southwest side of North Platte, broke off 10 inch diameter tree limbs, and tore siding off a home.  As the storm moved northeast of town, it tore siding and shingles off a home north of the airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwesterly winds in the upper levels of the atmosphere triggered isolated thunderstorms with hail and strong winds over western and central Nebraska during the afternoon and evening hours.

A cluster of thunderstorms developed near the Columbus area during the early afternoon hours of July 9th.  The storms developed along a weak nearly stationary front aided by high instability in the vicinity and a weak upper level disturbance.  The storms drifted slowly south into early evening.  Besides a few of them producing strong winds and large hail, some also dumped locally heavy rain.  The rain was especially heavy just to the west of the region where some 5 to 6 inch amounts were reported in a several hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarter-size fell in the Tobias area for about 20 minutes.  The hail was accompanied by very heavy rain with around 2 inches reported in an hour or so.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwesterly flow aloft and abundant low level moisture led to severe thunderstorms across portions of central and southwestern Nebraska during the late morning and early afternoon hours.
Multicellular thunderstorms developed during the evening hours and produced hail and strong winds.

An increasing low level jet brought moist and unstable air into eastern Nebraska during the early morning hours of July 5th.  Slow moving severe thunderstorms developed as a result an hour or so before sunsrise.  The activity gradually weakened after mid morning as warmer air aloft spread over the region capping the atmosphere and shutting down the precipitation.  Besides producing pockets of large hail over the area, localized rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches were reported in a few hours time which caused isolated flash flooding and river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel-size hail was reported near Lyons.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center that stalled over northeast Colorado and the associated warm front across northern Kansas resulted in showers and thunderstorms into southwest Nebraska May 23, 2008. The persistent southeast surface wind flow provided deep moisture across southwest Nebraska that fueled training thunderstorms resulting in increased runoff from saturated ground and flash flooding. In addition, thunderstorms developing in an unstable atmosphere produced isolated large hail.

An approaching cold front and upper level disturbance encountered rich low level moisture, resulting in the development of supercell thunderstorms across portions of southwest into north central Nebraska.

Supercell thunderstorms developed along a nearly stationary front across southwest Nebraska during the afternoon hours of June 4th and continued through the afternoon of June 5th.  The storms produced very large hail during the overninght and morning hours, then some tornadoes during the afternoon of June 5th.
A warm front that lifted north into southern Nebraska was the focus for evening thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds.
Scattered thunderstorms across western Nebraska produced hail and heavy rain.
Thunderstorms developed by mid morning along a stationary front with severe storms redeveloping by early afternoon in an increasingly unstable environment.

As an upper level low pressure system tracked along the United States and Canadian border, a weakening cold front pushed across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  Ahead of the front temperatures climbed into the upper 70s and dewpoint temperatures reached the lower to mid 60s.  The resultant instability and cold front helped to spawn severe thunderstorms across southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa, with hail up to 1 inch in diameter the primary severe parameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust was measured at 59 mph by the Falls City Asos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures and ample moisture from southeast winds provided the ingredients necessary for isolated thunderstorms to produce hail across central and north central Nebraska during the evening hours.

Thunderstorms formed in a moist, unstable airmass with grounds already near saturation that led to flash flooding in east central Holt County as over 3 inches rain fell as thunderstorms moved northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain with 4 inches of rain falling on already saturated ground. The heavy rain led to flash flooding with numerous roads under water.  Roads inundated with water include gravel roads throughout Holt County, Old County Road 108 east of O'Neill, Highway 281 south of O'Neill, and County Road S45A north of Page.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across the region during the afternoon and evening of May 29th followed by a weak cool front passage later that night.  As the warm front passed, temperatures warmed into the lower to mid 80s and dewpoint temperatures climbed into the 65 to 70 degree range.  The whole system was aided by an intense upper level low pressure area that moved from the eastern Rockies into the northern plains.  The combination of the high instability in place, the 2 fronts and the upper level disturbance created conditions that were favorable not only for severe thunderstorms, a few of which were supercells that produced tornadoes, but also areas of very heavy rainfall that produced flash flooding that night and river and stream flooding that lasted a day or two later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts, which were estimated at close to 60 mph, downed a few large tree limbs in Lyons which downed a power line and broke a power pole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced some hail in Furnas, Webster and Dawson counties of south-central Nebraska.

A stationary front over Kansas started lifting north on June 4th as an unseasonably strong upper level low pressure system was organizing over the central Rockies.  This allowed very warm and unstable air to feed north into southern Nebraska and southwest Iowa during the afternoon on the 4th.  Severe thunderstorms that developed by early afternoon on June 4 over central Nebraska quickly spread and developed into eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa through evening.  A few tornadic producing supercells developed over the area...and many of the storms contained very heavy rain which fell on soils that were already saturated from significant rains recorded over the prior week.  This caused areas of flash flooding and subsequent river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado started about 4 miles southwest of Ulysses in Seward county, crossed into Butler county about 1.5 miles south of Ulysses and then lifted around 1 mile east southeast of town.  The tornado flipped irrigation center pivots along its path and caused tree and farm outbuilding damage.  It was rated an EF1 in both Seward and Butler counties.  The total path length in both counties was a little over 5 miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds in Dixon and Dakota Counties in extreme northeast Nebraska, from a little before midnight to just after midnight on the night of July 20th and 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused widespread tree damage, including numerous trees blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a cold front and moved southeast through the evening.  The storms produced large hail, strong wind gusts, heavy rain, and a brief tornado.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second tornado spun up while the first tornado to its south was dissipating.  This tornado affected mainly the northern end of Ceresco downing trees and power lines and causing some building damage.  This tornado could have also possibly traveled farther east, but the survey was limited due to downed power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface boundary draped across the region provided a focus for thunderstorm development as an upper level disturbance crossed the Central Plains.  These thunderstorms developed during the late afternoong and early evening hours, and conditions were favorable for thunderstorms to become severe.  Hail up to the size of quarters was reported across portions of south central Nebraska, along with wind gusts estimated up to 80 mph.  A low level jet nosing into the area helped to sustain convection through midnight, after which thunderstorms moved south out of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured by a Nebraska Department of Roads sensor at the junction of Highways 14 and 74.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: July 16, 2008 a surface boundary across the northwest became the focus for late afternoon severe thunderstorm development. Severe thunderstorms produced several reports of large hail with up to 3 inches in diameter falling in south central Cherry county.

Thunderstorms developed along an area of low pressure that extended northeast across southwest Nebraska by late afternoon. The thunderstorms initially produced a landspout tornado and large hail, but then transitioned into damaging winds as they moved eastward.

An approaching cold front and upper level disturbance encountered rich low level moisture, resulting in the development of supercell thunderstorms across portions of southwest into north central Nebraska.

An upper level trough continued to dig over the western United States, accompanied by a weak disturbance and jet streak nosing into the Central Plains.  A surface warm front draped across northern Kansas provided a focus for afternoon convection, aided by other surface boundaries across south central Nebraska from previous thunderstorms.  North of this front, conditions were favorable for widespread thunderstorm development, with numerous reports of severe weather across south central Nebraska.  Heavy rain also accompanied these storms, causing significant flooding.  Many locations had over 2 inches of rain with a few locations seeing 5 to 7 inches of rainfall. These storms continued on until the early morning hours, coming to an end as the upper level energy shifted east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail accompanied by wind gusts estimated at 70 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few hailstones approached baseball-size in the Surprise area which busted a few windshields.  The hail was from the same supercell thunderstorm that produced a tornado in the Ulysses area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of severe thunderstorms produced some large hail over parts of Scotts Bluff and Cheyenne counties of the Nebraska panhandle.

A cold front that moved across the area kicked off a relatively small complex of severe thunderstorms over east central and southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  Initially, as the front came through during the late afternoon hours, warm mid level temperatures limited coverage and strength of the activity.  However, around sunset mid level cooling, aided by a weak upper level disturbance, helped increase the strength of convection that was developing above the cap just to the west of the area.  These thunderstorms then tracked across the region and produced areas of damaging winds.  An isolated thunderstorm produced marginally severe-size hail well north of the complex several hours after they had weakened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 70 mph blew a well constructed shed down 5 miles west of Seward.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary stalled across far Southwestern Nebraska on the afternoon of the 24th.  As an upper level disturbance moved into Western Kansas, scattered thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and evening hours.  This activity moved north into Southwestern Nebraska, where it produced hail ranging in size from pennies to golf balls.

Scattered thunderstorms developed during the evening hours. A few of these storms produced some hail, mainly three-quarters of an inch in diameter. One storm did produce some quarter size hail in Superior as reported by the local law enforcement.

A warm front and low pressure system over northern Kansas lifted northeast into southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa as an unseasonably strong upper level low pressure system lifted from the Rockies into the northern plains.  The front and upper level disturbance caused fast moving thunderstorms, a few severe, to develop during the late morning hours in northeast Nebraska.  After a brief lull in the activity through early afternoon, additional thunderstorms developed over eastern Nebraska.  The storms developed along 2 lines, one extended north of Columbus while the other stretched from southeast Nebraska into southwest Iowa. Several of the storms turned severe, including one supercell that produced an EF2 tornado in extreme southeast Nebraska.  Besides the severe weather, a few of the thunderstorms produced additional areas of heavy rain that fell on saturated soils and caused some already high rivers to flood.

A surface cold front moving in from the northwest stalled out over the Central Plains, providing a focus for thunderstorm development across south central Nebraska.  Conditions were favorable for thunderstorms to become strong to severe, but capping aloft delayed development until the late afternoon hours.  Severe thunderstorms dropped hail to the size of quarters and golf balls in many area, even to the size of baseballs near Prosser in Adams County. The storms transitioned into heavy rain producers after dark with several reports of flash flooding.

A surface cold front crossing the Central Plains provided a focus for evening thunderstorm development across south central Nebraska.  Conditions ahead of the front were favorable for severe thunderstorms, with penny to half dollar size hail reported, along with heavy rain and flash flooding. Storms ended across the area during the late evening hours as the cold front shifted further east.

Thunderstorms developed along a trough of low pressure over the High Plains during the afternoon and evening hours, which gradually shifted east to impact portions of south central Nebraska.  Some of these thunderstorms became severe, with reports of hail to the size of quarters and wind gusts up to 76 mph.

Thunderstorms developed over south central Nebraska during the evening hours as an upper level disturbance crossed over the Central Plains.  Modest instability and shear allowed a few of these to become severe, dropping hail up to the size of golfballs.
Thunderstorms developed along a cold front and moved southeast through the evening.  The storms produced large hail, strong wind gusts, heavy rain, and a brief tornado.
A few severe thunderstorms moved across portions of Kimball and Morrill counties and produced a brief tornado as well as some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail reported from 1.0 to 1.5 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a cold front and moved southeast through the evening.  The storms produced large hail, strong wind gusts, heavy rain, and a brief tornado.
Thunderstorms developed by mid morning along a stationary front with severe storms redeveloping by early afternoon in an increasingly unstable environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail completed covered the ground.
